name: vector-index-tuning
description: Optimize vector index performance for latency, recall, and memory. Use when tuning HNSW parameters, selecting quantization strategies, or scaling vector search infrastructure.
capabilities: Benchmark HNSW parameters (M, efConstruction, efSearch) for optimal recall-latency tradeoffs
Recommend quantization strategies (INT8, Product Quantization, Binary) for memory reduction
Generate Qdrant collection configurations for different optimization targets
Estimate memory usage for various vector index configurations
Provide monitoring templates for search latency and recall tracking
Guide index type selection based on data size and performance requirements
author: sickn33
securitySummary: Static analysis flagged 26 patterns that are all false positives. The skill contains only documentation and Python code examples for vector database optimization. Markdown code fences were misidentified as shell execution. URLs are reference links. Configuration parameter names were misidentified as filesystem operations. No actual security risks exist.
valueStatement: Vector search systems often struggle with latency and memory at scale. This skill provides proven HNSW tuning patterns and quantization strategies to balance recall, speed, and resource usage.
limitations: Provides guidance and code templates but does not execute benchmarks directly
Recommendations are based on general patterns and may require validation with your workload
Does not design end-to-end retrieval systems beyond index tuning
Requires workload metrics and ground truth data to validate recall improvements

useCases.targetUser: ML Engineers managing production vector search systems
useCases.title: Production Search Latency Optimization
useCases.description: Tune HNSW parameters and enable INT8 quantization to reduce p99 latency from 50ms to 10ms while maintaining 95% recall.

useCases.targetUser: Infrastructure Engineers optimizing cloud resource costs
useCases.title: Memory-Constrained Index Deployment
useCases.description: Apply Product Quantization to fit 10M vectors in 8GB RAM with acceptable recall tradeoffs for cost-sensitive deployments.

useCases.targetUser: Tech Leads planning search infrastructure growth
useCases.title: Vector Index Scaling Planning
useCases.description: Select appropriate index type and configuration when scaling from 100K to 100M vectors with predictable performance.

promptTemplates.title: Basic HNSW Parameter Recommendation
promptTemplates.prompt: I have 500,000 vectors with 768 dimensions. I need 95% recall at p99 latency under 20ms with 16GB memory budget. Recommend HNSW parameters and quantify expected memory usage.
promptTemplates.scenario: Getting started with HNSW tuning for a medium-scale deployment

promptTemplates.title: Quantization Strategy Selection
promptTemplates.prompt: Compare INT8 scalar quantization vs Product Quantization for my use case: 10M vectors, 512 dimensions, must fit in 8GB RAM, minimum 90% recall required. Include code to implement the recommended approach.
promptTemplates.scenario: Choosing between quantization methods for memory-constrained deployment

promptTemplates.title: Qdrant Collection Configuration
promptTemplates.prompt: Generate a complete Qdrant collection configuration optimized for high-recall search on 5M product embedding vectors. Include HNSW settings, quantization config, and optimizer thresholds with explanations.
promptTemplates.scenario: Production Qdrant setup for e-commerce semantic search

promptTemplates.title: Performance Benchmarking Plan
promptTemplates.prompt: Design a benchmarking plan to evaluate HNSW parameter sweeps. I have 1M vectors, 10K query samples with ground truth labels. Include metrics to track, parameter ranges to test, and criteria for selecting the winning configuration.
promptTemplates.scenario: Systematic performance evaluation before production deployment

bestPractices: Benchmark with real production queries rather than synthetic data to capture actual workload patterns
Start with default HNSW parameters and only tune when metrics indicate a need for optimization
Monitor recall continuously in production as data drift can degrade search quality over time
antiPatterns: Reindexing production systems without a rollback plan and staging validation
Over-optimizing for recall at the expense of latency SLAs and memory budgets
Skipping index warmup after deployment which causes cold-start latency spikes

faq.question: What is a good starting point for HNSW parameters?
faq.answer: Start with M=16, efConstruction=100, efSearch=50. These defaults work well for most datasets under 1M vectors. Only tune after measuring baseline performance against your latency and recall targets.

faq.question: How does quantization affect search quality?
faq.answer: INT8 scalar quantization typically causes 1-3% recall degradation with 75% memory savings. Product Quantization can achieve 10-20x compression but may degrade recall by 5-15%. Always validate recall impact with your specific dataset.

faq.question: When should I use IVF instead of HNSW?
faq.answer: Consider IVF when scaling beyond 100M vectors or when memory is severely constrained. HNSW offers better recall and latency for datasets under 100M vectors. IVF requires more tuning but can be more memory-efficient at extreme scale.

faq.question: How often should I reindex my vector database?
faq.answer: Reindex when recall degrades beyond your threshold (typically 2-5% drop), after significant data distribution changes, or when scaling infrastructure. Monitor recall metrics continuously to trigger reindexing proactively.

faq.question: What is the difference between efConstruction and efSearch?
faq.answer: efConstruction controls index build quality (higher = better index, slower build). efSearch controls query quality (higher = better recall, slower search). You can use high efConstruction once during build, then tune efSearch for your latency-recall tradeoff.

faq.question: Can I change HNSW parameters after creating an index?
faq.answer: efSearch can be changed dynamically per query. However, M and efConstruction are fixed at index creation time. Changing these requires rebuilding the index with new parameters.
